citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The licensee is not using appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. APHIS
inspectors observed one (1) adult Asian elephant, Betty, with a stiff gait in the right front forelimb, as evidenced by
decreased ability to flex the wrist (carpal) joint as compared to the opposite limb. Betty was observed flexing the left wrist
approximately 75 degrees, while only flexing the right wrist roughly 10 degrees when walking. At the time of inspection,
the stiffness was observed to be slightly worse when the elephant gave rides to the public. The licensee acknowledged
that the elephant is lame in that limb. The attending veterinarian (AV) was aware of the lameness/stiffness and had
prescribed an anti-inflammatory medication to be used as needed. However, the lameness visible at the time of inspection
indicates that the licensee is not using the prescribed medication at a frequency and/or dosage that is effective at
controlling and treating this condition.
Failure to adequately treat conditions in a timely manner can lead to unnecessary distress, pain, and negatively affect the
animal's health and well-being. Each dealer or exhibitor shall establish and maintain programs of adequate veterinary
care that include the use of appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. Correct by
ensuring that all animals are receiving adequate veterinary care including the use of appropriate methods to prevent,
control, diagnose, and treat disease and injuries.
Correct by Oct. 14, 2025

The licensee is not handling all animals in a manner that does not cause trauma, behavioral stress, physical harm, or
unnecessary discomfort. APHIS inspectors observed Betty, an approx. 56-year-old elephant, walking with a stiff gait, and
inspectors observed that this lameness increased when the licensee allowed members of the public to ride the elephant.
Betty’s walking speed decreased, and the stiffness was more clearly observed. The licensee told inspectors that the
elephant’s “stiffness” increases when she is ridden by members of the public, but that “she warms out of it.” Allowing
members of the public to ride her is causing additional lameness resulting in trauma, physical harm, or unnecessary
discomfort.
Handling of all animals shall be done as expeditiously and carefully as possible in a manner that does not cause trauma,
overheating, excessive cooling, behavioral stress, physical harm, or unnecessary discomfort. Correct by ensuring that
animals are handled as expeditiously and carefully as possible in a manner that does not cause trauma, physical harm, or
unnecessary discomfort.
Correct by end of business Oct. 1, 2025
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
The inspected elephants were Betty and Janice at the New River Valley Fairgrounds in Dublin, VA.
Additional Inspectors:
Jessica Siegal-Willott n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
The licensee is not using appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. APHIS 
inspectors observed one (1) adult Asian elephant, Betty, with a stiff gait in the right front forelimb, as evidenced by 
decreased ability to flex the wrist (carpal) joint as compared to the opposite limb. Betty was observed flexing the left wrist 
approximately 75 degrees, while only flexing the right wrist roughly 10 degrees when walking. At the time of inspection, 
the stiffness was observed to be slightly worse when the elephant gave rides to the public. The licensee acknowledged 
that the elephant is lame in that limb. The attending veterinarian (AV) was aware of the lameness/stiffness and had 
prescribed an anti-inflammatory medication to be used as needed. However, the lameness visible at the time of inspection 
indicates that the licensee is not using the prescribed medication at a frequency and/or dosage that is effective at 
controlling and treating this condition.  
 
Failure to adequately treat conditions in a timely manner can lead to unnecessary distress, pain, and negatively affect the 
animal's health and well-being. Each dealer or exhibitor shall establish and maintain programs of adequate veterinary 
care that include the use of appropriate methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries. Correct by 
ensuring that all animals are receiving adequate veterinary care including the use of appropriate methods to prevent, 
control, diagnose, and treat disease and injuries. 
Correct by Oct. 14, 2025 
2.131(b)(1)  Direct
   
Handling of animals. 
The licensee is not handling all animals in a manner that does not cause trauma, behavioral stress, physical harm, or 
unnecessary discomfort. APHIS inspectors observed Betty, an approx. 56-year-old elephant, walking with a stiff gait, and 
inspectors observed that this lameness increased when the licensee allowed members of the public to ride the elephant. 
Betty’s walking speed decreased, and the stiffness was more clearly observed. The licensee told inspectors that the 
elephant’s “stiffness” increases when she is ridden by members of the public, but that “she warms out of it.” Allowing 
members of the public to ride her is causing additional lameness resulting in trauma, physical harm, or unnecessary 
discomfort. 
 
Handling of all animals shall be done as expeditiously and carefully as possible in a manner that does not cause trauma,

overheating, excessive cooling, behavioral stress, physical harm, or unnecessary discomfort. Correct by ensuring that 
animals are handled as expeditiously and carefully as possible in a manner that does not cause trauma, physical harm, or 
unnecessary discomfort. 
Correct by end of business Oct. 1, 2025 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives. 
 
The inspected elephants were Betty and Janice at the New River Valley Fairgrounds in Dublin, VA. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
Jessica Siegal-Willott n
